topicArea: Political Philosophy, the Paradox of Tolerance & the Limits of Liberal Toleration

questions.passage:
A liberal society that commits itself to tolerating a wide range of beliefs and practices eventually confronts an uncomfortable possibility: some of the beliefs it must tolerate, if its commitment is truly unconditional, will themselves be committed to abolishing toleration for everyone else. A movement that seeks, quite openly, to use the freedoms a tolerant society extends to it in order to seize power and then eliminate those same freedoms presents no formal contradiction that a rule of unconditional toleration could invoke to exclude it. Extended far enough and consistently enough, unlimited toleration appears to permit its own eventual destruction, since a society that tolerates everything provides an intolerant faction every opportunity, and no principled basis for restriction, to grow strong enough to abolish toleration once and for all. This is the paradox of tolerance in its classic form, and it has resisted a clean resolution for as long as political philosophers have taken it seriously.

The most direct response denies that toleration can be unconditional in the first place. On this view, a liberal order is not merely permitted but obligated to withhold toleration specifically from movements whose explicit aim is toleration's abolition, restricting their speech, association, or political participation as a matter of principled self-defense rather than inconsistency. Call this the restrictive response: toleration for all except those who would destroy toleration for all.

This response encounters an immediate and serious objection. It requires some institution to determine, in advance and with some degree of confidence, which movements count as sufficiently intolerant to warrant restriction, and this determination is a power difficult to bound once granted. Nearly any strongly held political position can be characterized by its opponents as an existential threat to toleration itself, and a state empowered to suppress movements on this basis has been handed an instrument at least as dangerous, in the wrong hands, as the intolerance it was originally designed to prevent. The historical record of institutions granted this kind of discretionary suppressive power offers little reassurance, since such powers have frequently expanded well past the narrow category of genuinely existential threats they were first justified by.

A second response denies the paradox's underlying premise rather than accepting and then restricting toleration. On this view, toleration was never meant to function as a purely procedural commitment, indifferent to the content of what it tolerates. Toleration is itself a substantive value, inseparable from a prior commitment to respect for persons as free and equal participants in a shared social order. A movement dedicated to abolishing that respect for some category of persons is not, on this account, expressing one tolerable position among many that a consistent liberal must nonetheless accept; it is attacking the very foundation the commitment to toleration rests upon in the first place. Refusing to tolerate such a movement, on this view, involves no genuine inconsistency, since toleration was never a commitment to neutrality about human equality to begin with.

This second response faces its own difficulty, one that some critics find at least as troubling as the first. The move can look suspiciously convenient: it allows a liberal order to declare, essentially by definition, that whatever it wishes to suppress simply falls outside the category of things toleration was ever meant to protect, without offering any independent, non-circular test for locating that boundary. A critic excluded on these grounds could just as easily insist that his own view was never genuinely intolerant in the relevant sense, and at that point the dispute collapses into competing assertions about definitions rather than arriving at any principled resolution external to the disagreement itself.

A third response steps back from seeking a purely logical resolution altogether and treats the paradox as fundamentally a matter of institutional and empirical judgment rather than abstract principle. Rather than asking, in the abstract, whether intolerant views should be tolerated as a category, the practically relevant question becomes how much actual capacity a given movement currently has to translate its stated aims into effective power. Toleration, on this account, can be calibrated to that empirical threat level rather than resolved once and permanently by a single formal rule, which means accepting that the boundary of what gets tolerated will remain genuinely uncertain and subject to revision as circumstances change, rather than fixed by any theory in advance.

None of these three responses dissolves the underlying tension so much as trades one difficulty for a different one. The restrictive response risks handing a society an unbounded suppressive power justified by a judgment call that later hands may apply far more broadly than first intended. The substantive response risks resolving the paradox only by definitional fiat, redrawing toleration's boundary to exclude whatever a given order already wished to exclude. The practical response avoids both risks only by accepting permanent uncertainty about exactly where, at any given moment, the line currently sits.

That this paradox has resisted a clean resolution across a long tradition of political theory, without any of its major proposed responses managing to dissolve the tension entirely, suggests it may be less a puzzle awaiting a decisive solution than a permanent structural feature of what liberal toleration actually demands from any society serious enough to worry about the possibility of its own undoing.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Liberal societies should never restrict the speech or political participation of any group, regardless of that group's stated aims, since any restriction is indistinguishable from the intolerance it claims to prevent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each proposed resolution of the paradox of tolerance introduces a new difficulty of its own, suggesting the paradox is a permanent feature rather than a solvable puzzle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The restrictive response correctly resolves the paradox of tolerance, since toleration was never intended to protect movements aiming at toleration's own destruction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Institutions granted the power to restrict intolerant movements have historically expanded that power well beyond its original justification.

explanations.A: This choice draws a categorical conclusion far stronger than anything the passage endorses. The author presents the restrictive response as one serious position with real costs, not as a position to be rejected outright, and never argues that all restriction is indistinguishable from intolerance; the passage's structure treats this as one contested position among several, not as a settled conclusion.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage's central argument is that the restrictive, substantive, and practical responses to the paradox of tolerance each address the underlying tension, unconditional toleration seemingly permitting its own destruction, only by introducing a new difficulty of their own, and that the paradox's persistence across a long tradition of political theory suggests it may be a permanent feature of liberal toleration rather than a puzzle with a final solution.
explanations.C: This choice treats one of three contested responses as though the passage endorsed it as correct. The third paragraph raises a serious, unresolved objection to the restrictive response, which is inconsistent with treating that response as having successfully resolved the paradox.
explanations.D: This choice isolates a single supporting detail from the third paragraph and mistakes it for the passage's overall thesis. That historical observation supports the broader claim that each proposed response carries its own difficulty, but it is not, by itself, the passage's main point.

questions.stem: The primary function of the third paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: prove that no political movement has ever been correctly identified as a genuine threat to toleration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: argue that the restrictive response should be discarded entirely and replaced by the response the passage introduces in the following paragraph.

choices.label: C
choices.text: raise an objection to the restrictive response: the power to define intolerant movements is difficult to bound once granted.

choices.label: D
choices.text: demonstrate that historical institutions have never been granted the power to restrict political movements.

explanations.A: This choice overstates the paragraph's claim into an empirical assertion about every historical case. The paragraph does not argue that no movement has ever been correctly identified as a genuine threat; it argues that the power to make such determinations is difficult to bound once granted, a different and more limited claim.
explanations.B: This choice attributes a stronger recommendation to the paragraph than it makes. The third paragraph raises an objection to the restrictive response; it does not go on to argue that the response introduced in the fourth paragraph should replace it, since that response is itself subjected to its own objection in the fifth paragraph.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph's central move is to argue that granting any institution the power to determine which movements are sufficiently intolerant to warrant restriction creates a power that is difficult to bound, since nearly any strongly held position can be characterized by opponents as an existential threat, and such powers have historically tended to expand.
explanations.D: This choice contradicts the paragraph's actual claim, which discusses the historical tendency of such powers to expand once granted, presupposing that institutions have in fact been granted this kind of discretionary power, not that they never have been.

questions.stem: Based on the fourth paragraph, a proponent of the substantive response would most likely respond to the objection that this response allows a society to define away whatever it wishes to suppress by arguing that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: toleration was never neutral about human equality, so excluding movements attacking that equality is not arbitrary definition.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the objection is correct, and the substantive response should therefore be abandoned in favor of the practical response described in the sixth paragraph.

choices.label: C
choices.text: any movement restricted under the restrictive response should automatically be restricted under the substantive response as well.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the paradox of tolerance does not actually exist, since no society has ever genuinely committed itself to unconditional toleration.

explanations.A: Correct. The fourth paragraph's substantive response holds that toleration was never a purely procedural, content-neutral commitment but a substantive value tied to respect for human equality; a proponent would therefore argue that excluding a movement attacking that equality is not an arbitrary act of definition but a direct application of what toleration was always understood to protect.
explanations.B: This choice describes a proponent conceding the objection entirely, which is inconsistent with defending the substantive response. A proponent responding to the fifth paragraph's objection would defend the response's underlying logic, not agree that the objection succeeds and abandon the position for an alternative.
explanations.C: This choice introduces a claim about automatic overlap between the restrictive and substantive responses that the passage does not make. The two responses are presented as distinct approaches with different justifications and different objections; nothing suggests a proponent of one would claim automatic application of the other.
explanations.D: This choice denies that liberal societies have ever genuinely committed to unconditional toleration, which is not the substantive response's actual argument. The substantive response accepts that such a commitment exists but argues it was never meant to be neutral about human equality, rather than denying the commitment ever existed.

questions.stem: The phrase definitional fiat, as used in the passage, most nearly refers to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: settling a disputed boundary by declaring, without independent justification, that certain views fall outside protection.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a formal legal ruling issued by a national court with the final authority to interpret constitutional protections for political speech.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a historical treaty between two governments establishing mutual toleration of religious minorities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: an empirical measurement of how much political power a given movement currently holds.

explanations.A: Correct. Definitional fiat describes settling a genuinely disputed boundary simply by declaring, without any independent or non-circular justification, that certain views fall outside a protected category, which is precisely the concern the fifth paragraph raises about the substantive response's boundary between tolerable and intolerable views.
explanations.B: This choice narrows the phrase to a specific institutional mechanism, a court ruling, that the passage does not describe. Definitional fiat refers to the underlying logical move of declaring a boundary by assertion rather than to any particular formal legal procedure.
explanations.C: This choice introduces a historical concept, a treaty between governments, that has no connection to the passage's use of the phrase. Definitional fiat concerns how a conceptual boundary is drawn within a philosophical argument, not a specific diplomatic agreement.
explanations.D: This choice describes something closer to the practical response's empirical approach, measuring a movement's actual capacity for power, which the passage explicitly contrasts with drawing a boundary by fiat rather than equates with it.

questions.stem: A liberal democracy passes a law restricting the political activities of any group whose official platform calls for the abolition of free elections. Critics of the law argue that determining which platforms meet this description will inevitably become a matter of political judgment subject to abuse by whichever party controls the relevant institutions. This criticism most directly illustrates the objection the passage raises against:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the substantive response, since the fifth paragraph's objection concerns whether toleration was ever a purely procedural commitment rather than the abuse of institutional power.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the practical response, since the sixth paragraph explicitly endorses calibrating toleration to a movement's current empirical capacity for seizing power.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the restrictive response, whose grant of power to identify intolerant movements the third paragraph argues is hard to bound.

choices.label: D
choices.text: none of the three responses, since the passage does not discuss laws restricting political parties specifically.

explanations.A: This choice misidentifies which response the criticism targets. The fifth paragraph's objection to the substantive response concerns whether toleration was ever a purely procedural, content-neutral commitment, a definitional question, not the specific worry about institutional power being abused, which is the third paragraph's concern about the restrictive response.
explanations.B: This choice misapplies the criticism to the practical response, which the sixth paragraph presents as explicitly embracing calibration to empirical political power rather than claiming to avoid the risk of institutional abuse through a fixed legal category, making it a poor match for this particular criticism.
explanations.C: Correct. The law described restricts political activity based on an institutional determination of which platforms count as sufficiently anti-democratic, precisely the kind of discretionary power the third paragraph warns is difficult to bound once granted, since the criteria for restriction can be characterized broadly by whichever institution applies them.
explanations.D: This choice incorrectly claims the scenario falls outside the passage's discussion. Restricting a political movement's activities based on its stated platform is a direct instance of the kind of institutional power the third paragraph specifically discusses in relation to the restrictive response.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the objection raised against the restrictive response in the third paragraph?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that most citizens support restricting the political activities of movements openly committed to abolishing free and regular elections.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that movements committed to abolishing toleration are relatively rare in most liberal democracies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that legal scholars disagree about the precise definition of a political movement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that institutions granted narrowly defined, judicially reviewed restriction authority have not expanded it across many documented cases.

explanations.A: This choice concerns public opinion about restriction, which does not address whether the power to restrict, once granted, tends to expand beyond its original scope. Popular support for a specific restriction says nothing about the institutional dynamics the third paragraph's objection concerns.
explanations.B: This choice concerns how common intolerant movements are, which does not address whether an institution granted restrictive power would apply that power narrowly or expand its use over time. Rarity of intolerant movements does not bear on the objection about unbounded institutional discretion.
explanations.C: This choice concerns an unrelated definitional disagreement among legal scholars about what counts as a political movement, which does not address whether restrictive power, once granted to an institution, tends to expand beyond its original justification.
explanations.D: Correct. The third paragraph's objection depends on the claim that discretionary restrictive power, once granted, is difficult to bound and tends to expand. Documented evidence that a class of institutions operating under narrow, judicially reviewed criteria has restricted only movements meeting those criteria, without measurable expansion, would directly weaken the objection by showing the feared expansion does not necessarily occur.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the passage's claim that the substantive response's boundary between tolerable and intolerable views risks being drawn by definitional fiat?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A finding that most citizens are unable to accurately describe the substantive response in their own words when surveyed about toleration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A finding that theorists applying the substantive response's own criteria reach sharply divergent classifications of the same movements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A finding that the restrictive response is more commonly invoked by governments than the substantive response.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A finding that movements historically classified as intolerant have tended to be smaller in membership than movements classified as tolerable.

explanations.A: This choice concerns public understanding of the substantive response as a theory, which does not address whether the boundary the response draws between tolerable and intolerable views is itself independently justified or merely asserted. Public comprehension of a theory is unrelated to whether its central distinction is principled.
explanations.B: Correct. The fifth paragraph's objection is that the substantive response's boundary may be drawn by definitional fiat rather than by any independent, non-circular test. Evidence that theorists applying the response's own stated criteria reach sharply divergent conclusions about the same movements would suggest the criteria do not actually yield a determinate, principled answer, directly supporting the claim that the boundary risks being drawn arbitrarily.
explanations.C: This choice concerns the relative frequency with which governments invoke the restrictive response compared to the substantive response, which does not address whether the substantive response's own boundary is principled or merely asserted by fiat.
explanations.D: This choice concerns the size of movements historically classified as intolerant, which does not address whether the substantive response's classification criteria are independently justified. Membership size is unrelated to whether the underlying boundary is drawn by definitional fiat.
